USD.catalyst: The dollar leads G10 on a hawkish pivot at the Fed under new Chair Kevin Warsh, who has dropped the dovish bias from official communications; markets now price at least one further 25bp hike this year, lifting expectations above the current 3.50%-3.75% range. PCE inflation forecasts were revised sharply higher to 3.6%, pushing easing further out. Renewed US-Iran tensions have driven energy prices and safe-haven flows into the dollar, while resilient US growth -- underpinned by an AI-led tech boom and a tight labor market -- continues to outperform other regions.

GBP.catalyst: Sterling is supported by a structural yield advantage as the Bank of England holds Bank Rate at 3.75% even as other G10 central banks have moved toward cuts, keeping carry attractive against the euro, Swiss franc and yen. MPC member Taylor has signaled an extended hold to keep fighting domestic inflation, limiting near-term rate-cut pricing. Investor perception of UK fiscal policy has remained stable, with limited concern over fiscal slippage, reinforcing confidence in the currency.

sources.title: BoE's Taylor backs extended hold on interest rates

EUR.catalyst: The ECB delivered a 25bp hike on 11 June, lifting the deposit, refi and marginal lending rates to 2.25%/2.40%/2.65%, citing inflation pressure from the Middle East war; staff now see headline inflation averaging 3.0% in 2026 even as the growth outlook was trimmed to 0.8%. The move marks a genuine hawkish turn after a long hold, narrowing the policy gap with the Fed and supporting the euro on rate-differential grounds. Eurozone growth remains uneven -- resilient in Spain and France, flat in Germany and Italy -- but the bloc's institutional stability and the ECB's inflation-fighting credibility continue to anchor investor confidence in the currency.

sources.title: Monetary policy decisions -- European Central Bank

JPY.catalyst: The Bank of Japan raised its policy rate 25bp to 1.00% on 16 June, the highest level since 1995, with the board voting 7-1 in favor as inflation concerns outweighed growth risks. Wholesale inflation hit 6.3% in May, the highest since 2023, driven by oil prices tied to the US-Iran conflict, while the yen had weakened past 160 per dollar despite earlier intervention by Japanese authorities. Deputy Governor Himino reiterated on 24 June that the BoJ will keep tightening as underlying inflation risks staying above target, and board member Tamura has floated a path toward a 2% neutral rate -- a hawkish signal that narrows the BoJ's policy gap with other G10 central banks and supports the yen.

sources.title: Bank of Japan hikes rates to 1%, highest since 1995, as yen and inflation worries take hold

AUD.catalyst: The RBA left the cash rate unchanged at 4.35% in June, pausing after three consecutive hikes earlier in 2026 as it assesses the impact of prior tightening on a slowing economy. Governor Bullock said inflation is still too high and remains a central concern, with the Board focused on preventing the recent oil-driven price impulse from becoming embedded. Markets are pricing roughly even odds of one further hike later this year, and major banks are split on the timing of any eventual cut, leaving the Aussie's rate path more uncertain than most G10 peers.

sources.title: Statement by the Monetary Policy Board -- RBA Media Releases
RBA pauses on rates for first time this year but doesn't rule out more hikes

CAD.catalyst: The Bank of Canada held its overnight rate at 2.25% on 10 June for a fifth consecutive meeting, balancing a soft domestic economy -- GDP contracted in Q1 and unemployment sits near 7% -- against fresh inflation risk from Middle East-driven energy prices, which pushed headline CPI to 2.8% in April. Governor Macklem described the situation as a genuine dilemma for policy, with the Bank keeping the door open to both a cut, if US trade restrictions worsen, and further hikes, if energy-driven inflation broadens. As a major net oil exporter, Canada's terms of trade benefit from elevated crude prices even as consumers face higher costs at the pump.

sources.title: Bank of Canada maintains the policy rate at 2 1/4%

CHF.catalyst: The SNB held its policy rate at 0% on 18 June, as expected, judging current conditions sufficient to support both price stability and growth even as Middle East-linked energy costs lift the inflation outlook modestly. The Bank reiterated it sees room to intervene directly in currency markets to manage franc strength rather than move rates, reflecting persistent safe-haven demand for the franc during periods of geopolitical stress. With inflation forecasts still anchored below 1% through 2027, the SNB remains the most dovish G10 central bank by a wide margin, even as the franc continues to draw flows on risk-aversion grounds.

sources.title: Switzerland Interest Rate -- Swiss National Bank

NZD.catalyst: The RBNZ held the Official Cash Rate at 2.25% at its 29 May meeting, but the decision was split three-three, with Governor Breman casting the deciding vote, and the central bank's updated projections show rates running meaningfully higher through 2026-27 than previously signaled. Breman has said rates are likely to rise sooner and by more than earlier guidance indicated, as Middle East-linked energy costs push near-term inflation toward a projected 4.3% peak. The hawkish shift in tone, even without an immediate hike, has been read by markets as RBNZ effectively closing the door on further easing and opening one toward tightening, supporting the kiwi against lower-yielding peers.

sources.title: RBNZ recap: Rates may need to rise sooner and faster

NOK.catalyst: Norges Bank held its policy rate at 4.25% on 17 June after an out-of-consensus hike in May, but flagged that capacity utilization, while drifting down, still leaves room for another increase if inflation proves stickier than projected. The Committee is watching whether a potential reopening of the Strait of Hormuz eases energy-driven price pressure, but Governor Wolden Bache has stressed elevated business costs are likely to keep inflation high regardless. With the policy rate already among the highest in the G10 and forecasts pointing modestly above 4.5% by year-end, NOK continues to carry one of the widest yield advantages in the bloc.

SEK.catalyst: The Riksbank held its policy rate at 1.75% on 17 June, the fifth straight hold, but raised the probability of a hike later this year, citing growing inflation risk from prolonged Middle East supply disruptions even as the 2026 inflation forecast was cut to 0.6%. Policymakers described current Swedish economic activity as weak, with disinflation running well below target through the first half of the year, leaving the Riksbank notably more dovish than Norges Bank despite facing a similar energy-price shock. The krona remains the most rate-disadvantaged Nordic currency in the near term, with any hawkish repricing now contingent on the conflict's duration.
